hints.Noun: 'Beati' is used as a predicate adjective here, describing the 'pauperes'
hints.Verb: 'Beati' is not a verb — there is no finite verb in this clause (the copula 'sunt' is implied)
hints.Adverb: An adverb would modify a verb — 'Beati' describes the subject 'pauperes'

hints.Genitive: Genitive plural would be 'beatorum' — 'Beati' is nominative plural masculine
hints.Vocative: Vocative plural would also be 'beati', but this is a statement, not an address

hints.Accusative: 'pauperes' could be accusative plural, but here it is the subject — nominative

hints.1st: 1st declension nouns end in -a — 'pauper' with genitive 'pauperis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension nouns end in -us/-um — 'pauper' is a 3rd declension adjective/noun
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns end in -us with genitive -us — 'pauper' has genitive 'pauperis'

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'spiritus' is 'spiritus' — the -u ending is ablative in 4th declension
hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'spiritus' is 'spiritus' (with long -u) — the short -u is ablative

hints.2nd: 2nd declension -us nouns have ablative in -o — 'spiritu' with -u ending is 4th declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension nouns have ablative in -e — 'spiritu' has the 4th declension -u ablative
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es (e.g. 'res') — 'spiritus' is 4th declension

translation: Blessed are the poor in spirit
